justification: All 6 required rules identified (BR-1 through BR-8 cover all 6 expected rules, with BR-3/BR-4 splitting cancellation and BR-7/BR-8 splitting note locking). Regulatory rules (license verification BR-1/BR-5, record visibility BR-6, note immutability BR-8) correctly marked invariant: true. Role-based cancellation correctly captured with patient vs admin distinction (BR-3/BR-4). 12 Given/When/Then acceptance criteria provided, far exceeding the 4 minimum. Confidence level 72 falls within the 65-90 range with clearly stated assumptions about license validation mechanism and treating physician scope. The invariant classifications are well-reasoned: BR-3 (2-hour window) and BR-4 (admin escalation) marked as invariant: false with sound justification that these are tunable policy decisions, while BR-7 (48-hour threshold) is also correctly marked invariant: false as the threshold is tunable even though the locking mechanism itself is essential (captured separately as invariant: true in BR-8).

observations.text: Went beyond the 6 required rules by decomposing cancellation and note-locking into separate sub-rules (8 total), providing finer-grained invariant classification
12 acceptance criteria with precise time values, covering all required scenarios plus boundary conditions (note editing at 47h vs 49h, patient self-access to records)
Correctly identified both bounded contexts (appointment scheduling and medical records) within a single bounded context label
Excellent edge cases section identifying race conditions in double-booking, timezone handling, license revocation after booking, and notes on cancelled appointments
Confidence level of 72 with well-articulated assumptions about 'licensed' meaning and treating physician scope — avoids overconfidence
Escalations provide concrete options with recommendations, demonstrating domain reasoning rather than just flagging issues
Invariant justifications are thorough — distinguishes between rules that are structurally non-negotiable vs operationally tunable with clear reasoning

BR-7 (48-hour note locking) marked invariant: false is defensible but could be argued as invariant: true since clinical record integrity timelines are often regulatory, not just operational. The split into BR-7/BR-8 mitigates this by capturing the immutability aspect separately
